text: Zebrafish serves as a model organism for hearing and deafness. Mutations in several genes connected to development of inner ear affect morphology and patterning of the inner ear epithelium, including formation of the semicircular canals and, in some, development of sensory patches (maculae and cristae). Zebrafish mutant embryos fail to balance correctly, and may swim on their sides, upside down, or in circles (Whitfield et al., 1996). This is reminiscent of the behavior of deaf mouse mutants, which often display hyperactive circling or head bobbing due to vestibular dysfunction (Whitfield, 2002).    Dog-eared mutants show abnormal development of semicircular canals and lack cristae within the ear (Kozlowski et al., 2005), while in van gogh, semicircular canals fail to form altogether, resulting in a tiny otic vesicle containing a single sensory patch. Both mutants show irregular swimming pattern (Whitfield et al., 1996).
